abstract:
### Abstract

**Background:** Diabetic nephropathy (DN) represents a critical microvascular complication of diabetes characterized by progressive glomerular and tubular damage. While the anthraquinone derivative rhein is known to possess nephroprotective properties, its precise molecular targets in DN remain poorly defined. This study investigates the impact of rhein on ferroptosis and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in DN and explores the involvement of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling axis.

**Methods:** We established in vitro and in vivo models using high glucose (HG)-treated mouse podocytes (MPC5) and streptozocin (STZ)-induced diabetic C57BL/6J mice. Various assays, including CCK-8, flow cytometry for ROS, and biochemical analysis of iron, MDA, GSH, and SOD, were used to assess cellular health and ferroptosis. Gene and protein expressions were quantified via RT-qPCR, Western blotting, and immunofluorescence. Histological changes in renal tissues were evaluated using HE and Masson staining.

**Results:** Rhein treatment significantly improved viability in HG-challenged podocytes and ameliorated renal dysfunction and fibrosis in DN mice. Molecular analysis revealed that rhein suppressed HG-induced ferroptosis—marked by reduced lipid peroxidation, Fe²⁺ accumulation, and ACSL4/TFR1 expression—while restoring GPX4 and SLC7A11 levels. Furthermore, rhein inhibited the EMT process by downregulating Vimentin, Snail, and α-SMA while preserving E-cadherin and Nephrin. These protective effects were associated with the suppression of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin axis. Mechanistically, Rac1 silencing mimicked the protective effects of rhein, whereas Rac1 overexpression neutralized rhein’s ability to inhibit ferroptosis and EMT, promoting oxidative damage and podocyte loss.

**Conclusions:** Rhein attenuates the progression of diabetic nephropathy by inhibiting ferroptosis and EMT through the modulation of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway. These findings highlight rhein as a potential therapeutic agent targeting podocyte stability and redox homeostasis in diabetic kidney disease.
